contract.name: v2/bulk_download/status.md
contract.description: Documentation describes a JSON job-status response, but every observed request to https://api.usaspending.gov/api/v2/bulk_download/status closes after TLS with no HTTP status or body.

inputSchema.description: No HTTP exchange completed; parameter handling remains unverified. Documentation suggests a file_name query string, but that behavior is unconfirmed.

outputSchema.description: Server never emits an HTTP response; actual payload and status codes are unknown.

quirks: HTTPS connections to api.usaspending.gov complete TLS and then terminate with an empty reply (curl error 52).
The www.usaspending.gov front-end host responds to the same path with cached HTML instead of the documented JSON payload.
risks: Clients cannot distinguish success or failure because the API aborts the connection before sending HTTP status information.
Integrations may mistakenly target www.usaspending.gov and receive HTML responses that break JSON parsers.
gaps: No successful responses observed; request parameters and response schema remain undetermined.
Authentication, header requirements, and error surface are unknown.

probes.notes: HTTPS call terminated with curl error 52 (Empty reply from server).
HTTPS call without file_name parameter closed without HTTP response (curl error 52).
Base host probe shows api.usaspending.gov closes TLS connections even for the root path (curl error 52).
www.usaspending.gov responds to the API path with static HTML instead of JSON.

mismatches: Published contract promises a 200 JSON payload, but api.usaspending.gov closes the connection without emitting any HTTP response.
gaps: No evidence confirming the documented file_name query parameter or any alternative inputs.
Response structure, status codes, and error handling remain unknown because the service never replies.
risks: Client libraries will raise low-level connection errors (curl error 52 / RemoteDisconnected) instead of actionable HTTP failures.
Routing requests through www.usaspending.gov yields HTML that can silently corrupt downstream JSON consumers.
